secac__001.text:
Plain English Summary of Research:
Background: Gum disease (periodon//s, 6th most common human disease) is a chronic inflamma/on that affects the gums and destroys the bone (gum pockets) that keeps the teeth in the jaws. It is a significant public health problem and a source of social inequality with high financial cost. When periodon//s is not treated, it may advance further and eventually it causes tooth loss, which has nega/ve effect on speech, nutri/on and wellbeing. Treatment is aimed at reducing gum pockets and re-establishing gum health. Following treatment in secondary care specialist/Level 2 centres, pa/ents should be followed up at NHS primary care regularly over life/me as part of a “suppor/ve periodontal care” (SPC) plan provided by the general dental prac//oner (GDP) and dental team (hygienists and nurses). SPC aims to regularly assess and mo/vate for good oral hygiene, provides tooth cleaning and intervenes if signs of gum disease re-appear. SPC is essen/al to save teeth, as relapse of gum disease may happen soon aTer comple/on of specialist treatment without appropriate SPC. While research has shown that regular, good quality SPC can maintain gum health for a long /me, even in high-risk pa/ents, and there are interna/onal validated guidelines on the frequency and type of SPC procedures, the content of SPC is not well regulated within NHS and is oTen open to individual GDP interpreta/on. This may result in inadequate SPC care for the pa/ents, with the risk of disease relapse and compromised long-term gum health.
Aims: The study aims to: 1) assess the feasibility to create a primary care network of general dental prac/ces in East London and East Anglia; 2) to iden/fy barriers related to the implementa/on of SPC programmes in primary care dental prac/ces; 3) to elaborate a strategy/interven/on (with an upskilled dental team) to address the iden/fied barriers and 4) to assess the feasibility of implemen/ng it in a limited number of pilot prac/ces (6-8 prac/ces). This la]er aim will provide pilot clinical data on disease relapse, plaque and inflammatory scores of pa/ents that follow the current NHS standard level of care as compared to pa/ents treated by the upskilled team.

Detailed Research Plan:
Design and methods: This feasibility study consists of 4 main projects that will take place in two main centres (Barts Health and East Anglia). The first step of the study consists in the crea/on of a network of GDPs in East London and East Anglia. Following the comple/on of an audit at the Periodontology Department of Royal London Dental Hospital/Barts Health, we have already iden/fied GDPs that regularly refer periodontal pa/ents. These “champion” prac/ces and other prac/ces in East London that are expected to refer periodontal pa/ents to the secondary care centre in Barts Health will be contacted by phone with the aim to recruit 3-4 prac/ces and create an ini/al network. Likewise, the Essex Professional Primary Care Network will contact GDPs amongst their network to iden/fy another 3-4 prac/ces to recruit.
The second step consists in the iden/fica/on of barriers and enablers to implement SPC in primary care and will be achieved through structured interviews with the dental team belonging to the recruited prac/ces (hygienists, den/sts and recep/onists) as well as periodontal pa/ents, informed by the Consolidated Framework for Implementa/on Research (CFIR). We will recruit at least one den/st, one hygienist and one recep/onist from each dental prac/ce, who must be involved in the administra/ve and clinical management of pa/ents with periodontal disease. We will also recruit 15-20 periodontal pa/ents who have received or are about to start SPC.
The list of all barriers and enablers iden/fied will be used to develop an online ques/onnaire, which will be sent to all par/cipa/ng GDPs asking clinical teams and pa/ents to indicate how important they believe each factor is to implement SPC successfully in primary care. Responses will be captured using 5-point ordinal scales and to allow ranking responses and iden/fying priori/es.
In the third step of the study we will design a specific interven/on that addresses those barriers (e.g. lack of /me, lack of training of the dental team, lack of knowledge by the pa/ents) and that should aim to improve SPC in primary care. In the last step of the study we will test this interven/on in the recruited prac/ces in the form of a 12-month randomized feasibility study. More specifically, prac/ces will be randomized either to the control or test group. While the control prac/ces will not receive any interven/on, the GDPs/dental teams randomized to the interven/on group, will be offered dedicated training to upskill the whole dental team on validated SPC (see Phase I). The approach will be to use didac/c ac/vi/es that support ac/ve learning, experien/al learning and structured reflec/ve prac/ce examining the role of teams to facilitate a change in SPC delivery. Specifically, we will combine classroom learning with prac/cal projects. Phase I will u/lise classroom style teaching alongside printed educa/on materials. Also, a novel approach will be used by a health psychologist/behavioural scien/st addressing human behaviour and the psychology of change. Phase II will u/lise experien/al learning involving a trained research hygienist who will train, observe and conceptualise ac/vi/es with the whole dental team at their prac/ce.

Patient & Public Involvement:
Pa6ent and public involvement: Pa/ents taking part in a focus group run at the Centre for Oral Clinical Research felt that SPC at their GDP were not of appropriate frequency nor mo/va/ng. The project was seen as value for money and was suggested that the NHS should invest more in oral health as it affects the wellbeing of people. The pa/ents will help designing the protocol, they will contribute to study mee/ngs and will be involved in the dissemina/on of the results.

Other Content:
Dissemina6on: it will be done by repor/ng in journals, at conferences, on our webpages, and via social media, podcasts and pa/ents’ forum.

It is a significant public health problem and a source of social inequality with high financial cost. Treatment is aimed at reducing gum pockets and re-establishing gum health. Following treatment in secondary care specialist/Level 2 centres, pa/ents should be followed up at NHS primary care regularly over life/me as part of a “suppor/ve periodontal care” (SPC) plan provided by the general dental prac//oner (GDP) and dental team (hygienists and nurses). SPC aims to regularly assess and mo/vate for good oral hygiene, provides tooth cleaning and intervenes if signs of gum disease re-appear. SPC is essen/al to save teeth, as relapse of gum disease may happen soon aTer comple/on of specialist treatment without appropriate SPC. While research has shown that regular, good quality SPC can maintain gum health for a long /me, even in high-risk pa/ents, and there are interna/onal validated guidelines on the frequency and type of SPC procedures, the content of SPC is not well regulated within NHS and is oTen open to individual GDP interpreta/on. This may result in inadequate SPC care for the pa/ents, with the risk of disease relapse and compromised long-term gum health.

This la]er aim will provide pilot clinical data on disease relapse, plaque and inflammatory scores of pa/ents that follow the current NHS standard level of care as compared to pa/ents treated by the upskilled team.

Detailed Research Plan:
Design and methods: This feasibility study consists of 4 main projects that will take place in two main centres (Barts Health and East Anglia). The first step of the study consists in the crea/on of a network of GDPs in East London and East Anglia. Following the comple/on of an audit at the Periodontology Department of Royal London Dental Hospital/Barts Health, we have already iden/fied GDPs that regularly refer periodontal pa/ents. These “champion” prac/ces and other prac/ces in East London that are expected to refer periodontal pa/ents to the secondary care centre in Barts Health will be contacted by phone with the aim to recruit 3-4 prac/ces and create an ini/al network. Likewise, the Essex Professional Primary Care Network will contact GDPs amongst their network to iden/fy another 3-4 prac/ces to recruit.
The second step consists in the iden/fica/on of barriers and enablers to implement SPC in primary care and will be achieved through structured interviews with the dental team belonging to the recruited prac/ces (hygienists, den/sts and recep/onists) as well as periodontal pa/ents, informed by the Consolidated Framework for Implementa/on Research (CFIR). We will also recruit 15-20 periodontal pa/ents who have received or are about to start SPC.
